3.why: We need the length to loop over the array.
3.invariant: arr is still in its original order.

4.why: It will store each number's 1-bit count â€” the key we sort by.

5.happening: Starting the counting index i at 0.
5.why: We count the numbers left to right.
5.invariant: i marks the number currently being counted.

6.happening: Counting the 1-bits of the number at index {i}.
6.why: Every number needs its 1-bit count before we can sort.
Phase 1 is done; the sort can begin.

7.why: We destroy a copy while counting, leaving arr untouched.

8.happening: Resetting count to 0 for this number.
8.invariant: No bits counted yet for arr[{i}].

9.happening: num ({num}) still has at least one 1-bit left.
9.why: Brian Kernighan's loop runs once per set bit, not per bit position.

9.happening: num reached 0 â€” this number has {count} one-bit(s).
9.why: Every set bit has been cleared, so the count is final.

10.happening: Clearing the lowest set bit of num ({num}) with num & (num - 1).
10.why: Subtracting 1 flips the lowest 1 and the zeros below it; the AND erases exactly that bit.
10.invariant: Each iteration removes one 1-bit from num.

11.happening: One 1-bit was just cleared, so count rises to {count + 1}.
11.why: The number of iterations equals the number of set bits.
11.invariant: count tracks how many 1-bits have been cleared.

12.why: This count is the primary sort key for this number.

13.happening: Advancing to the next number to count.
13.why: Repeat until every number has a count.

14.happening: Counts ready ({bit_counts}); resetting i to 0 for the sort.
14.why: Selection sort starts again from the front.
14.invariant: arr is still unsorted; bit_counts matches it.

15.happening: Choosing which element belongs at position {i}.
15.why: Selection sort fixes one position per outer pass.

15.happening: Every position is filled â€” the array is fully sorted.
15.why: The sorted prefix now spans the whole array.
15.invariant: arr is sorted by (1-bit count, value).

16.happening: Assuming arr[{i}] = {arr[i]} is the smallest-key element so far.
16.why: We start each pass by guessing the current slot is best.
16.invariant: best points at the best candidate found so far.

17.why: Everything before i is already sorted; only the right side is searched.

18.happening: Scanning index {j} for a smaller key than best.
18.why: We must inspect every unsorted element to find the true minimum.
18.invariant: best is the smallest key in [{i}..{j}-1].

18.happening: Scan complete â€” the smallest key is at index {best}.
18.why: Every unsorted element has been compared.
18.invariant: best now holds the minimum for this pass.

19.why: Fewer 1-bits means a strictly smaller key.
19.invariant: A new minimum has been found.

19.happening: arr[{j}] does NOT have fewer 1-bits than best ({bit_counts[best]}).
19.why: So it can only win on a tie â€” checked next.

20.why: It has the smallest key seen so far.

21.happening: arr[{j}] ties best with {bit_counts[j]} one-bit(s) â€” break by value.
21.why: Equal 1-bit counts are ordered by the integer value.

21.happening: arr[{j}] has MORE 1-bits than best â€” skip it.
21.why: A larger key can never be the minimum.

22.happening: arr[{j}] = {arr[j]} is smaller than arr[{best}] = {arr[best]} â€” it wins the tie.
22.why: On equal 1-bits, the smaller value sorts first.

22.happening: arr[{j}] = {arr[j]} is not smaller than arr[{best}] = {arr[best]}.
22.why: The current best already sorts earlier.

23.happening: arr[{j}] becomes the new best (tie broken by smaller value).
23.why: Same 1-bit count, smaller value â€” it sorts earlier.

24.happening: Moving the scan to the next index.

25.why: A three-step swap needs a temporary holder.
25.invariant: temp preserves the value currently at i.

26.why: The smallest-key element takes its final sorted spot.
26.invariant: Position {i} now holds the pass minimum.

27.happening: Putting the saved value into position {best} â€” swap done.
27.why: Completes the exchange between i and best.
27.invariant: arr[{i}] and old arr[{best}] have traded places.

28.why: The key row must swap in lockstep with arr.

29.why: So the key under each number stays correct.
29.invariant: The key row mirrors the value swap.

30.happening: Completing the key swap at position {best}.
30.why: Both the value and its key are now in place.
30.invariant: bit_counts is aligned with arr again.

31.happening: Position {i} is settled â€” advancing to the next.
31.why: The sorted prefix grows by one.

32.why: Every element is ordered by 1-bit count, then value.

init.happening: Setting up the counters before counting bits.
init.why: We need an empty key list and a start index.

loop.happening: Iterating â€” over numbers (phase 1) or positions (phase 2).
loop.why: Loops drive both the counting and the sort.
loop.invariant: The loop index marks current progress.

update.happening: Updating a count, a best candidate, or swapping elements.
update.why: These are the state changes the animation shows.
update.invariant: State stays consistent with the algorithm.

move.happening: Advancing a pointer to the next element/position.

check.happening: Comparing 1-bit counts, or values on a tie.
check.invariant: best tracks the current minimum key.
